Author bio: Content creator | AI Systems builder | Zero-headcount believer

Created: 2026-06-14T13:06:47.000Z

Article content: {"plain_text":"Most AI agents wait for you to type. Hermes Agent does not. And it does something none of the others do: it gets smarter overnight.
Between 11PM and 8AM, a properly configured Hermes setup monitors systems, ingests knowledge, completes scheduled work, refines its own skills, and lands a briefing in your Telegram by morning. The self-improvement loop runs in a background fork. The Curator prunes stale procedures. The Kanban dispatcher fires every 60 seconds. Cron jobs detect changes for free and only spend tokens when something actually happens.
The infrastructure costs $7-30/month. The output compounds every night.
This article maps the full 9-hour overnight cycle hour by hour, the infrastructure that makes it possible, what you review when you wake up, how to troubleshoot when something breaks, the security layers that keep autonomous operation safe, and the realistic timeline for what you actually get out of it — from Day 1 to Month 1.
All technical details verified against Hermes Agent v0.16.0 "The Surface Release" (June 5, 2026) official documentation.
 
WHY "WHILE YOU SLEEP" IS NOT MARKETING
Most agents are reactive. You type, they respond. They sit idle the rest of the time.
Hermes is architected differently. Three properties make overnight operation real:
→ Persistent gateway process (runs as system service, never sleeps) → Cron daemon with 60-second tick (executes scheduled work) → Self-improvement loop (refines skills after completed tasks)
This is not "AI that pretends to work overnight." It is concrete infrastructure running concrete jobs against concrete data.
 
Full 17-layer architecture mapping that powers this overnight operation is on my substack: https://substack.com/@yanxbt
 
THE 24-HOUR TIMELINE
23:00 — SESSION CLOSE
Your last conversation of the day ends. The session closes cleanly. SessionDB persists conversation state. Memory loop scans the day's exchanges, extracts durable facts, writes to MEMORY.md and USER.md (capped at ~800 and ~500 tokens respectively).
Nothing dramatic. Just clean cleanup.
23:30 — SELF-IMPROVEMENT LOOP
Completed tasks from the day are reviewed in a background fork. Patterns that worked get saved as skills in ~/.hermes/skills/. The agent does not announce this. You wake up to find new procedures in your skill library.
 
The Self-improvement Loop (Loop 3) is one of 8 nested loops running in parallel.
00:00 — CURATOR CHECK
Every 7 days, the Curator wakes between sessions. Scans ~/.hermes/skills/, identifies redundant or stale procedures, archives unused skills to .archive/ (recoverable). Hub-installed skills stay off-limits. The library stays clean without manual work.
02:00 — COMPETITIVE INTEL CRON
A cron job fires. A Python script scrapes competitor pricing pages, diffs against last week's snapshot. If nothing changed: {"wakeAgent": false} — zero LLM tokens spent. If something changed: agent wakes, reads the diff, writes a summary to your wiki, drafts a Telegram alert for the morning.
 
The $0 gate that decides whether the LLM should wake up at all.
03:00 — KNOWLEDGE INGESTION
LLM Wiki ingest cron runs. Hermes ships with a bundled llm-wiki skill based on Andrej Karpathy's LLM Wiki pattern: a self-improving knowledge base built as interlinked markdown files.
Unlike RAG (which rediscovers knowledge from scratch every query), the wiki compiles knowledge once and keeps it current. Cross-references stay linked. Contradictions get flagged automatically.
The overnight cron pulls articles you saved during the day from a watch folder, indexes them into your Obsidian-compatible wiki, cross-references against existing entries, and updates any affected pages. Set WIKI_PATH in ~/.hermes/.env (defaults to ~/wiki).
By morning, your knowledge base has grown. The agent knows more than it did when you went to sleep — not just about you, but about your world.
04:00 — SCHEDULED REPORTS
Weekly performance reviews, monthly billing summaries, daily uptime reports. Most use no_agent mode (pure Python scripts, zero LLM cost). Output streams to Telegram or Slack via gateway REST endpoints.
06:00 — MORNING BRIEFING PREP
A cron job assembles your morning briefing. Pulls overnight cron results, Kanban board state, new wiki entries, top calendar items for the day, anything flagged urgent. Draft goes through the agent (this one needs reasoning).
07:00 — KANBAN DISPATCHER
The Kanban dispatcher has been running every 60 seconds all night. Zombie task detection, heartbeat tracking, retry budgets. Any tasks in "Ready" state get assigned to available workers.
08:00 — BRIEFING LANDS
Your Telegram pings. Morning brief: 5 bullets max. What changed overnight. What needs your attention. What's on the calendar. Token cost so far this month.
You pour coffee. Read the brief. Decide what matters.
 
WHAT YOU REVIEW IN THE MORNING
The whole point of overnight automation is that mornings should be short. Five surfaces, 7-10 minutes total.
1. TELEGRAM BRIEFING (2 min)
The morning brief lands at 08:00. Format that works:
→ Top 3 urgent items (flagged [urgent] [today] [this week]) → Overnight changes that need a decision → Calendar highlights for today → Token spend this month (running total) → Anything that triggered wakeAgent overnight
Tap reply if you need the agent to act on something immediately. Tap a Kanban link if a task needs your input.
2. KANBAN BOARD (2 min)
hermes dashboard → Kanban
Scan four columns:
→ Blocked — your input required. Handle these first. → In Progress — what's running now. Confirm priorities. → Ready — what dispatcher will pick up next. → Done — what completed overnight. Skim, archive, move on.
The dispatcher runs every 60 seconds. Anything you move to Ready gets picked up within 60 seconds.
3. NEW SKILLS REVIEW (1-2 min)
hermes skills --new
Lists skills created in the last 24 hours by the self-improvement loop. Two questions for each:
→ Is the skill accurate? → Should it run automatically (default) or require approval?
Edit the markdown file directly if you want to refine wording or add restrictions. Bad skills get reused if you don't catch them early.
4. WIKI ADDITIONS (1-2 min)
ls ~/wiki/*.md -lt | head
Recent entries from overnight ingest. Cross-references already built by the agent. Skim for:
→ New entries you flagged for follow-up → Contradictions the agent surfaced → Topics that grew large enough to need a parent page
5. /USAGE CHECK (30 sec)
/usage
Token spend today, this week, this month. Compare against your budget. If a single overnight cron burned more than expected, that's a configuration issue worth investigating before the next night.
THE MORNING SHORTCUT COMMAND
Set this in your SOUL.md or as a skill:
/morning
Custom command that runs all five reviews and outputs one concise summary. Define it once, use it every day.
Most days: 7-10 minutes total. Bad days: 15-20 minutes (something needs real attention). The goal is to spend less time reviewing than the work would have taken if you did it yourself.
 
THE INFRASTRUCTURE THAT MAKES THIS WORK
Five components running persistently:
→ VPS or local machine running 24/7Hetzner CX22 (~$7/month) handles the load. Local Mac Mini works but breaks if you lose power.
→ Gateway as system serviceRuns through systemd (Linux), launchd (macOS), or Hermes's installed-service mode. Survives reboots.
→ Cron daemon (60s tick)Built into the gateway. Fires scheduled jobs.
→ Persistent storage~/.hermes/ holds sessions, memory, skills, kanban DB. Survives restarts. Backups are just file copies.
→ At least one messaging platformTelegram is fastest to set up. Discord and Slack work the same.
 
SOUL.md restrictions become hard rules during autonomous overnight runs. Without clear restrictions, the agent makes unpredictable choices when you are not there to review.
 
DESKTOP APP CHANGES THE MORNING WORKFLOW
v0.16.0 "The Surface Release" (June 5, 2026) shipped a native Electron app for macOS, Linux, and Windows. Built across 100 PRs in a single week.
For the "while you sleep" setup specifically:
→ Connect to a remote Hermes gatewayYour VPS runs Hermes 24/7. Your Desktop app on your laptop connects via OAuth or username/password. Same memory, skills, sessions — accessed from wherever you are.
→ Concurrent multi-profile sessionsReview what your research agent did, your code agent did, your ops agent did — in separate tabs simultaneously.
→ In-app self-updateHermes updates itself without you SSHing into the VPS.
→ Drag-and-drop filesDrop overnight outputs back into the chat for analysis.
The Desktop app does not replace the gateway running on your VPS. It is a control surface for it.
 
HOW TO SET UP "WHILE YOU SLEEP" MODE WITHOUT MISTAKES
Most overnight problems come from setup choices, not from running operations. Five configurations that prevent issues before they start.
1. SET HARD TOKEN CAPS BEFORE FIRST CRON FIRES
In config.yaml:
 
The agent stops when it hits the limit. A misconfigured cron job can spend $X but not $X+1. Set these BEFORE you create cron jobs, not after a surprise bill.
2. USE WAKE AGENT FOR EVERY MONITORING CRON
Default monitoring jobs to wakeAgent mode, not full agent runs. Script detects change for free. Agent only fires when something actually happened.
/cron add "every 1h" \
  --script check-something.py \
  --prompt "[only runs if script says wakeAgent: true]"
Rule of thumb: if a cron job runs more than once per hour, it should have a wakeAgent gate.
3. CONFIGURE CHECKPOINTS BEFORE LETTING THE AGENT TOUCH FILES
 
The agent snapshots your directory before changes. /rollback restores state. Without checkpoints enabled, you cannot undo what the agent did overnight.
4. WRITE RESTRICTIONS INTO SOUL.md BEFORE ENABLING AUTONOMY
The agent will do what you let it do. The restrictions section of SOUL.md is your defense. Be specific:
## Restrictions
Never deploy to production without me approving the diff.
Never run `rm -rf` or destructive commands.
Never spend more than $5 on a single API call.
Never send messages to anyone except via my approved channels.
Never modify files outside ~/projects/ without confirmation.
Never push to a git remote autonomously.
Vague restrictions ("be careful with my data") get ignored. Specific ones get followed.
5. SET APPROVAL MODE TO SMART FOR SENSITIVE PROFILES
 
Smart mode uses an auxiliary model to classify risk. Risky actions arrive on Telegram with Approve/Reject buttons. You stay in control of decisions that matter while the agent handles everything routine.
THE ORDER OF SETUP MATTERS
Run setup in this order to avoid expensive mistakes:
→ Day 0: Install Hermes, write SOUL.md with restrictions → Day 0: Set budget caps in config.yaml → Day 0: Enable checkpoints → Day 1: Connect messaging platform (Telegram) → Day 1: Test with manual tasks (no cron yet) → Day 2: Add ONE simple cron job (morning brief) → Day 3-7: Verify it runs cleanly for a week → Week 2: Add monitoring crons with wakeAgent gates → Week 3+: Expand based on what worked
Most expensive mistakes happen when people skip Day 2-7 and create 10 cron jobs on day one. The agent has no memory, no context, no restrictions tested, no proven track record. Then it runs unsupervised for 8 hours.
Start small. Verify. Expand.
 
OVERNIGHT TROUBLESHOOTING
Five things that break in real setups. Each with the fix.
"BRIEFING DIDN'T ARRIVE"
Check the gateway:
hermes gateway status
If the gateway crashed overnight, cron jobs that depend on REST endpoints to messaging platforms keep running (bot tokens work directly). Cron jobs that need the agent to compose a message fail silently.
Fix: run gateway as a systemd service (Linux) or launchd (macOS) so it auto-restarts on crash.
"CRON FIRED BUT NOTHING HAPPENED"
hermes cron list
hermes logs --since 12h
Common causes:
→ Script timeout (default 120s). Long-running scripts get killed. → wakeAgent gate returned false when it should have returned true. Test the script manually. → API key expired. Check hermes doctor.
"AGENT DID SOMETHING I DIDN'T EXPECT"
/rollback        # restore last file checkpoint
/rollback 3      # go back 3 checkpoints
Hermes snapshots files before changes when checkpoints are enabled. Restore the directory state. Then update SOUL.md restrictions to prevent the action next time.
"TOKEN SPEND IS WAY HIGHER THAN EXPECTED"
/usage
hermes prompt-size
Three common causes:
→ SOUL.md grew too large (every turn pays for it) → A cron job is wakeAgent=true when it should be wakeAgent=false → Sub-agent delegation went deep (each child = own session cost)
"KANBAN TASKS STUCK IN RUNNING"
The dispatcher detects zombie workers automatically (every 60 seconds). But if a task has been "Running" for hours with no heartbeat, manually reclaim it:
hermes kanban reclaim <task_id>
Or pause the dispatcher and investigate:
hermes kanban pause
hermes kanban resume
TELEGRAM, SLACK, OR DISCORD
Telegram is the fastest to set up. Discord and Slack work the same — same gateway, same commands, same cron delivery. Pick the one your team already lives in.
TELEGRAM (fastest setup)
hermes gateway setup
# pick Telegram
# message @BotFather → /newbot → paste token
Best for: solo founders, quick personal access, voice messages, mobile-first workflow.
SLACK (best for teams)
hermes gateway setup
# pick Slack
# create app at api.slack.com/apps
# install to workspace, paste tokens
Best for: shared agent across team members, threads as work history, integration with existing channels.
For cron delivery to specific Slack channels:
--deliver slack:#engineering
--deliver slack:@username
DISCORD (best for communities)
hermes gateway setup
# pick Discord
# create application at discord.com/developers
# paste bot token, invite to server
Best for: communities, voice channels (agent can join live voice calls), gaming/creative workflows.
MULTI-PLATFORM DELIVERY
A single cron job can deliver to multiple platforms:
/cron add "every day 8am" \
  --prompt "Morning brief" \
  --deliver telegram \
  --deliver slack:#ops
The agent generates one response. Both platforms receive it.
 
SECURITY: WHAT STOPS THE AGENT FROM DOING SOMETHING BAD OVERNIGHT
This matters more than people think. The agent runs while you sleep. Five layers of protection.
LAYER 1 — SOUL.md RESTRICTIONS
The first defense. Hard rules in SOUL.md become hard rules during autonomous runs:
## Restrictions
Never send messages on my behalf without approval.
Never deploy to production without running tests.
Never modify the database schema autonomously.
Never spend more than $X on a single API call.
These get scanned for prompt injection on load. Strong rules pass. Manipulation attempts get flagged.
LAYER 2 — APPROVAL GATES
Configure Hermes to require approval for sensitive actions:
 
Smart mode uses an auxiliary model to classify risk. Approval requests arrive on your home channel (Telegram by default) with Approve/Reject buttons.
LAYER 3 — CHECKPOINTS
 
Before Hermes touches files, it snapshots the directory. /rollback restores the state. The agent cannot permanently break anything in a checkpointed directory.
LAYER 4 — TOKEN BUDGET CAP
 
If you set a hard cap, the agent stops when it hits the limit. A runaway cron job can spend $X but not $X+1.
LAYER 5 — DOCKER OR VPS ISOLATION
Running Hermes in Docker or on a separate VPS limits blast radius. If something goes wrong, the agent cannot reach your local files, your local browser, or your local credentials. It only sees what you mounted into the container or VPS.
The combination of these five layers means autonomous overnight operation is safe enough to actually use. Without them, you would wake up worried every morning. With them, you wake up curious.
The compounding is real but measurable on a normal timeline.
DAY 1 — INITIAL SETUP
→ Gateway installed and running → Telegram connected → 1-3 cron jobs (morning brief, basic monitoring) → Empty skill library beyond the built-ins → Empty MEMORY.md → SOUL.md = starter template
You wake up to a basic brief. Useful but not impressive. The agent knows almost nothing about you yet.
WEEK 2 — KNOWLEDGE ACCUMULATING
→ 5-10 cron jobs running (competitor check, daily summary, weekly review) → 8-12 self-created skills from completed tasks → MEMORY.md has 1500-2000 chars of accumulated facts → Wiki has 20-50 indexed entries → Morning briefings reference YOUR specific projects, deadlines, preferences
First moment when you think: "okay, this is different."
MONTH 1 — COMPOUNDING VISIBLE
→ 12-15 cron jobs (most production-quality) → 20-30 self-created skills → Memory full, USER.md profile dialed in → Wiki has 100-200 entries with cross-references → Curator has run 4 times, pruned 5-10 stale skills → Briefings start including patterns YOU did not notice → The agent suggests cron jobs you did not think to add
Day 30 agent makes decisions Day 1 agent could not make. Same model, same setup. Different output quality because the system accumulated context.
 
REALISTIC OVERNIGHT COSTS
Token math for a typical "while you sleep" setup:
→ 5 wakeAgent cron jobs running through the night → Most fire on wakeAgent: false (zero LLM cost) → 1-2 fire on actual changes (5-15K tokens each) → Morning briefing generation (10-20K tokens) → Self-improvement loop background work (background fork, separate cache)
Realistic overnight token spend: 30-60K tokens.
At Claude Sonnet pricing: ~$0.20-0.40 per night. At GPT-5.5 via Codex (included): $0.
Plus $7/month VPS = total infrastructure cost $7-25/month depending on model choice.
A virtual assistant doing the same work costs $500-2000/month. The math is uncomfortable for traditional staffing.
 
SETUP CHECKLIST
Six steps to "while you sleep" mode:
Deploy VPS (Hetzner CX22, ~$7/month)
Install Hermes via one-command install script
Run hermes setup --portal for fastest model + tools + gateway path
Set SOUL.md with clear restrictions (the agent will do things while you are gone)
Connect Telegram as home channel for briefings
Add 3 starter cron jobs — morning brief, competitor scan, weekly review
That gets you to Day 1. Week 2 happens by using it. Month 1 happens because the system keeps running.
 
THE COMPOUNDING POINT
The reason "while you sleep" is not a gimmick: every overnight cycle adds something the next cycle can use.
A new skill from yesterday accelerates a task next week. A memory entry from this morning sharpens tomorrow's brief. A wiki entry from Tuesday catches a contradiction on Thursday.
A reactive AI tool resets every conversation. Hermes resets nothing. The state on Day 30 is the accumulated result of Days 1-29 of small, mostly invisible improvements.
This is what "the agent that grows with you" actually means in practice. Not marketing copy. A measurable difference between Day 1 output and Day 30 output, on the same prompts, with the same model.
